subject: Contracts
coverage_standard: NCBE-oriented expanded full bank including UCC Article 2 core rules

rules.title: Mutual Assent
rules.prompt_question: What is required for mutual assent in contract formation?
rules.rule_statement: A contract requires mutual assent, which is ordinarily shown by a valid offer and acceptance.
rules.application_example: If one party makes a definite proposal and the other clearly agrees, mutual assent is usually present.
rules.common_trap: Unexpressed subjective intent usually does not control if outward manifestations show agreement.

rules.title: Objective Theory of Assent
rules.prompt_question: How do courts determine whether assent exists?
rules.rule_statement: Courts generally determine assent under an objective standard by looking at the parties' outward words and conduct rather than undisclosed intent.
rules.application_example: A party who signs a contract is usually bound even if the party secretly meant something else.
rules.common_trap: Exams often tempt you to focus on hidden intent instead of objective manifestations.

rules.title: Offer
rules.prompt_question: What is an offer?
rules.rule_statement: An offer is a manifestation of willingness to enter into a bargain that justifies the offeree in understanding that acceptance will conclude the deal.
rules.application_example: A seller's definite promise to sell a car for a fixed price to a named buyer is usually an offer.
rules.common_trap: Preliminary negotiations are not offers if further assent is contemplated.

rules.title: Advertisements
rules.prompt_question: Are advertisements usually offers?
rules.rule_statement: Advertisements are usually invitations to deal rather than offers unless they are specific and leave nothing open for negotiation.
rules.application_example: A general newspaper ad usually is not an offer, but a reward ad may be.
rules.common_trap: Do not treat ordinary price quotes and ads as offers unless they are exceptionally definite.

rules.title: Termination by Lapse
rules.prompt_question: When does an offer terminate by lapse?
rules.rule_statement: An offer terminates by lapse after the time stated in the offer or, if no time is stated, after a reasonable time.
rules.application_example: An offer to sell fresh produce may lapse quickly because market conditions change fast.
rules.common_trap: Reasonable time depends on subject matter and circumstances.

rules.title: Termination by Rejection
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of rejection on an offer?
rules.rule_statement: A rejection by the offeree terminates the offer unless the offeror renews it.
rules.application_example: If the buyer says no to the seller's offer, the offeree generally cannot later accept unless the offer is renewed.
rules.common_trap: A rejection kills the offer even if the offeree changes mind later.

rules.title: Counteroffer
rules.prompt_question: What is a counteroffer and what is its effect?
rules.rule_statement: A counteroffer is a rejection of the original offer and a new offer by the offeree on different terms.
rules.application_example: If the buyer responds, I will buy for $900 instead of $1,000, that is usually a counteroffer.
rules.common_trap: A mere inquiry is not necessarily a counteroffer.

rules.title: Option Contract
rules.prompt_question: What is an option contract?
rules.rule_statement: An option contract is a promise to keep an offer open for a stated period that is binding if supported by consideration or another recognized substitute.
rules.application_example: If a buyer pays $100 to keep a real estate offer open for 10 days, the offeror generally cannot revoke within that period.
rules.common_trap: A promise to keep an offer open is not binding without consideration unless another rule applies.

rules.title: Firm Offer Under UCC
rules.prompt_question: When is a merchant's offer irrevocable under the UCC?
rules.rule_statement: A merchant's signed written offer assuring that it will be held open is irrevocable for the time stated, or for a reasonable time if none is stated, not exceeding three months.
rules.application_example: A merchant seller's signed letter promising to hold the price open for 60 days is a firm offer.
rules.common_trap: Firm offers apply to sales of goods and do not require consideration.

rules.title: Revocation
rules.prompt_question: When is revocation effective?
rules.rule_statement: A revocation is effective when received by the offeree.
rules.application_example: If the offeror mails a revocation and the offeree has not yet received it, the offer may still be accepted.
rules.common_trap: Unlike acceptance under the mailbox rule, revocation is not effective on dispatch.

rules.title: Indirect Revocation
rules.prompt_question: What is indirect revocation?
rules.rule_statement: An offer is revoked indirectly when the offeree receives reliable information that the offeror has taken definite action inconsistent with an intent to enter the contract.
rules.application_example: If the offeree learns from a reliable source that the offeror already sold the property to someone else, the offer is revoked.
rules.common_trap: The information must be reliable and the action must be definite.

rules.title: Acceptance
rules.prompt_question: What is acceptance?
rules.rule_statement: Acceptance is an objective manifestation of assent to the terms of the offer in a manner invited or required by the offer.
rules.application_example: If the offer invites acceptance by promise and the offeree clearly promises, acceptance occurs.
rules.common_trap: The offeree must comply with the offer's required mode if one is stated.

rules.title: Silence as Acceptance
rules.prompt_question: When may silence operate as acceptance?
rules.rule_statement: Silence ordinarily is not acceptance unless the offeree takes benefits with reason to know compensation is expected, the parties so agree, or prior dealings make silence reasonable.
rules.application_example: A party who accepts services knowing payment is expected may be bound even without speaking.
rules.common_trap: Silence is the exception, not the rule.

rules.title: Mailbox Rule
rules.prompt_question: When is acceptance effective under the mailbox rule?
rules.rule_statement: Unless the offer provides otherwise, an acceptance sent by an authorized means is effective upon dispatch.
rules.application_example: A mailed acceptance generally forms the contract when mailed, not when received.
rules.common_trap: This rule does not apply to option contracts, revocations, rejections, or counteroffers in the same way.

rules.title: Acceptance of Option Contract
rules.prompt_question: When is acceptance of an option contract effective?
rules.rule_statement: Acceptance of an option contract is effective only when received by the offeror.
rules.application_example: Mailing acceptance on the last day is not enough if the offeror does not receive it in time.
rules.common_trap: Do not apply the mailbox rule to option contracts.

rules.title: Unilateral Contract
rules.prompt_question: How is a unilateral contract accepted?
rules.rule_statement: A unilateral contract is accepted by complete performance of the requested act.
rules.application_example: A reward offer is usually accepted only when the act is fully performed.
rules.common_trap: Beginning performance generally does not complete acceptance, but it may affect revocation.

rules.title: Beginning Performance in Unilateral Contract
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of beginning performance on an offer for a unilateral contract?
rules.rule_statement: Beginning the invited performance on a unilateral contract offer generally creates an option making the offer temporarily irrevocable, but acceptance requires complete performance.
rules.application_example: If a reward offer invites climbing a mountain, beginning the climb may prevent revocation, but acceptance occurs only upon completion.
rules.common_trap: Starting performance does not mean the contract is already fully accepted.

rules.title: Bilateral and Unilateral Contracts
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between bilateral and unilateral contracts?
rules.rule_statement: A bilateral contract is formed by exchange of promises, while a unilateral contract is formed when the offeree accepts by performance.
rules.application_example: A standard service agreement is bilateral, while a reward offer is usually unilateral.
rules.common_trap: Many exam fact patterns are bilateral unless the offer clearly requires performance only.

rules.title: Acceptance Under UCC
rules.prompt_question: How may an offer to buy goods be accepted under the UCC?
rules.rule_statement: An offer to buy goods may be accepted by a prompt promise to ship or by prompt shipment of conforming or nonconforming goods, unless the seller seasonably notifies that nonconforming goods are only an accommodation.
rules.application_example: A seller may accept by shipping the goods ordered.
rules.common_trap: Shipping nonconforming goods may be both an acceptance and a breach unless clearly sent as accommodation.

rules.title: Consideration
rules.prompt_question: What is consideration?
rules.rule_statement: Consideration is a bargained for legal detriment or benefit exchanged between the parties.
rules.application_example: A promise to paint a house in exchange for payment is supported by consideration on both sides.
rules.common_trap: A mere gift promise lacks consideration.

rules.title: Past Consideration
rules.prompt_question: Does past consideration support a contract?
rules.rule_statement: Past consideration does not support a contract because it was not bargained for in exchange for the promise.
rules.application_example: A later promise to pay someone for a favor already completed is usually unenforceable.
rules.common_trap: The act must be induced by the promise, not completed before it.

rules.title: Illusory Promise
rules.prompt_question: What is an illusory promise?
rules.rule_statement: An illusory promise is not consideration because it does not actually bind the promisor to do anything.
rules.application_example: A promise to buy all the goods I want is usually illusory without additional limiting language.
rules.common_trap: Requirements and output contracts are different because good faith supplies the obligation.

rules.title: Preexisting Duty Rule at Common Law
rules.prompt_question: What is the preexisting duty rule at common law?
rules.rule_statement: At common law, a promise to perform a duty already owed does not constitute consideration for a new promise.
rules.application_example: A contractor who demands more money to do the same work usually gives no new consideration.
rules.common_trap: Look for new or different performance if the promisor claims modification is supported.

rules.title: UCC Contract Modification
rules.prompt_question: Is consideration required to modify a sales contract under the UCC?
rules.rule_statement: Under the UCC, consideration is not required to modify a contract for the sale of goods if the modification is made in good faith.
rules.application_example: A seller and buyer may adjust price due to genuine market disruption if done in good faith.
rules.common_trap: Good faith is still required even without new consideration.

rules.title: Promissory Estoppel
rules.prompt_question: When is a promise enforceable under promissory estoppel?
rules.rule_statement: A promise is enforceable under promissory estoppel when the promisor should reasonably expect reliance, the promise induces actual reliance, and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement.
rules.application_example: An employer's promise causing a worker to relocate may be enforced if reliance was foreseeable and substantial.
rules.common_trap: Promissory estoppel is not the same as consideration, but may substitute for it.

rules.title: Restitution and Unjust Enrichment
rules.prompt_question: When is restitution available?
rules.rule_statement: Restitution is available to prevent unjust enrichment when one party confers a benefit on another under circumstances making retention of the benefit unjust.
rules.application_example: A contractor who partially performs under an unenforceable contract may recover the value of benefits conferred.
rules.common_trap: Restitution focuses on the benefit received, not always the contract price.

rules.title: Minor's Capacity
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of infancy on a contract?
rules.rule_statement: A contract entered by a minor is generally voidable at the minor's option, subject to liability for necessaries.
rules.application_example: A minor may disaffirm a nonessential purchase contract, but may owe for food or shelter.
rules.common_trap: The contract is not automatically void; it is usually voidable.

rules.title: Mental Incapacity
rules.prompt_question: When may mental incapacity make a contract voidable?
rules.rule_statement: A contract is voidable for mental incapacity if the person was unable to understand the nature and consequences of the transaction or was unable to act reasonably and the other party had reason to know.
rules.application_example: A party who lacked understanding due to severe impairment may avoid the contract if the test is satisfied.
rules.common_trap: Mere weakness or poor judgment is not enough.

rules.title: Duress
rules.prompt_question: When is a contract voidable for duress?
rules.rule_statement: A contract is voidable for duress when assent is induced by an improper threat leaving the victim no reasonable alternative.
rules.application_example: A threat of wrongful violence to force agreement is classic duress.
rules.common_trap: Hard bargaining alone is not usually duress.

rules.title: Undue Influence
rules.prompt_question: When is a contract voidable for undue influence?
rules.rule_statement: A contract is voidable for undue influence when unfair persuasion by a dominant party overcomes the will of a vulnerable party.
rules.application_example: A caregiver pressuring an elderly dependent to sign over property may create undue influence.
rules.common_trap: Look for both susceptibility and overpersuasion.

rules.title: Misrepresentation
rules.prompt_question: When is a contract voidable for misrepresentation?
rules.rule_statement: A contract is voidable when a party's assent is induced by a fraudulent or material misrepresentation on which the party justifiably relied.
rules.application_example: A buyer who was induced to purchase by a false statement about mileage may rescind if reliance was justified.
rules.common_trap: Opinion is not always misrepresentation unless special circumstances make reliance justified.

rules.title: Nondisclosure
rules.prompt_question: When may nondisclosure amount to misrepresentation?
rules.rule_statement: Nondisclosure may be equivalent to misrepresentation when a party has a duty to disclose, such as to correct a prior statement, correct a basic mistake, or where a relationship of trust exists.
rules.application_example: A seller who knows a prior statement is false and fails to correct it may be liable for nondisclosure.
rules.common_trap: Silence alone is not always actionable.

rules.title: Mutual Mistake
rules.prompt_question: When may a contract be avoided for mutual mistake?
rules.rule_statement: A contract may be avoided for mutual mistake when both parties were mistaken about a basic assumption that materially affects the agreed exchange, unless the party seeking relief bore the risk of the mistake.
rules.application_example: If both parties contract for a cow believed fertile but it is barren, the contract may be avoidable if the assumption was basic and material.
rules.common_trap: Risk allocation can defeat the defense.

rules.title: Unilateral Mistake
rules.prompt_question: When may unilateral mistake justify relief?
rules.rule_statement: Unilateral mistake may justify avoidance if the mistake concerns a basic assumption, has a material effect, the mistaken party did not bear the risk, and enforcement would be unconscionable or the other party knew or caused the mistake.
rules.application_example: A contractor's bid with an obvious clerical error may be rescinded if the other side knew of the error.
rules.common_trap: Unilateral mistake is harder to prove than mutual mistake.

rules.title: Impossibility
rules.prompt_question: When is performance excused by impossibility?
rules.rule_statement: Performance is excused by impossibility when an unforeseen event occurring after contract formation makes performance objectively impossible.
rules.application_example: A contract to sell a specific painting is discharged if the painting is destroyed without fault before delivery.
rules.common_trap: Mere increased cost is not impossibility.

rules.title: Impracticability
rules.prompt_question: When is performance excused by impracticability?
rules.rule_statement: Performance is excused by impracticability when an unforeseen event makes performance extremely and unreasonably difficult or expensive and the event was a basic assumption of the contract.
rules.application_example: A sudden embargo preventing shipment may excuse performance.
rules.common_trap: Difficulty must be extreme, not just inconvenient.

rules.title: Frustration of Purpose
rules.prompt_question: When is performance excused by frustration of purpose?
rules.rule_statement: Performance is excused by frustration of purpose when an unforeseen event substantially destroys the principal purpose of the contract and both parties understood that purpose.
rules.application_example: Renting a room to watch a parade may be discharged if the parade is canceled and that purpose was understood by both parties.
rules.common_trap: The purpose, not just profitability, must be destroyed.

rules.title: Illegality
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of illegality on a contract?
rules.rule_statement: A contract with illegal subject matter or purpose is generally unenforceable.
rules.application_example: A contract to commit a crime is unenforceable.
rules.common_trap: Courts usually leave the parties where they find them, subject to limited exceptions.

rules.title: Unconscionability
rules.prompt_question: When may a contract or clause be unenforceable for unconscionability?
rules.rule_statement: A court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause that was unconscionable at the time it was made, often involving unfair surprise and oppressive terms.
rules.application_example: A hidden clause imposing shocking fees in a take-it-or-leave-it consumer contract may be unconscionable.
rules.common_trap: Usually look for both procedural and substantive unfairness.

rules.title: Statute of Frauds
rules.prompt_question: What is the general Statute of Frauds rule?
rules.rule_statement: Certain contracts are unenforceable unless evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged.
keywords: signed by the party to be charged
rules.application_example: A contract within the Statute of Frauds generally needs a sufficient signed writing to be enforced.
rules.common_trap: The contract is not void; it is unenforceable absent satisfaction or an exception.

rules.title: Major Statute of Frauds Categories
rules.prompt_question: What contracts commonly fall within the Statute of Frauds?
rules.rule_statement: Common Statute of Frauds categories include marriage contracts, suretyship promises, contracts not performable within one year, contracts for interests in land, and contracts for the sale of goods for $500 or more under the UCC.
rules.application_example: A two year employment agreement ordinarily falls within the one year provision.
rules.common_trap: If full performance is possible within one year, the one year provision does not apply.

rules.title: One Year Provision
rules.prompt_question: When does the one year provision of the Statute of Frauds apply?
rules.rule_statement: The one year provision applies only if the contract cannot possibly be fully performed within one year from the date it was made.
rules.application_example: A lifetime employment promise does not fall within the one year provision because death could occur within a year.
rules.common_trap: The test is possibility, not probability.

rules.title: Land Contracts
rules.prompt_question: What land related contracts fall within the Statute of Frauds?
rules.rule_statement: Contracts for the sale of land or interests in land generally fall within the Statute of Frauds.
rules.application_example: A contract to sell a parcel of real estate ordinarily requires a sufficient signed writing.
rules.common_trap: Short leases may be treated differently under local rules, but bar analysis usually focuses on sale of land and land interests.

rules.title: Part Performance Exception for Land
rules.prompt_question: When may part performance satisfy the Statute of Frauds for a land contract?
rules.rule_statement: Part performance may permit enforcement of a land contract when the buyer's conduct clearly indicates the existence of the contract, such as payment, possession, and improvements.
rules.application_example: A buyer who pays, takes possession, and makes valuable improvements may enforce an oral land contract in equity.
rules.common_trap: Usually one act alone is not enough on bar exams; look for strong corroborating conduct.

rules.title: UCC Sale of Goods for $500 or More
rules.prompt_question: When does the UCC Statute of Frauds apply to a sale of goods?
rules.rule_statement: A contract for the sale of goods for $500 or more is generally unenforceable unless there is a sufficient signed writing indicating a contract for sale.
rules.application_example: An oral contract for sale of machinery worth $10,000 usually requires a sufficient writing or an exception.
rules.common_trap: The writing can be minimal under the UCC, but it must indicate a contract and quantity.

rules.title: Merchant Confirmatory Memo
rules.prompt_question: What is the merchant confirmatory memo rule under the UCC?
rules.rule_statement: Between merchants, a signed confirmatory memo sufficient against the sender binds the recipient if the recipient has reason to know its contents and does not object in writing within 10 days.
keywords: does not object in writing within 10 days
rules.application_example: One merchant's signed invoice confirming an oral order may satisfy the statute against the other merchant if no timely objection is made.
rules.common_trap: This rule applies only between merchants.

rules.title: UCC Statute of Frauds Exceptions
rules.prompt_question: What are important UCC Statute of Frauds exceptions?
rules.rule_statement: UCC exceptions include specially manufactured goods, admissions in pleadings or testimony, and goods paid for or accepted.
rules.application_example: If the buyer admits in court that an oral goods contract existed, the statute may be satisfied to that extent.
rules.common_trap: These exceptions are tested frequently.

rules.title: Parol Evidence Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the parol evidence rule?
rules.rule_statement: When the parties adopt a final written agreement, prior or contemporaneous agreements are not admissible to contradict it, and if the writing is complete, additional consistent terms are also barred.
rules.application_example: A party generally cannot introduce an earlier oral term that contradicts a final integrated writing.
rules.common_trap: The rule bars certain evidence only to vary the writing, not for all purposes.

rules.title: Parol Evidence Exceptions
rules.prompt_question: What are common exceptions to the parol evidence rule?
rules.rule_statement: Parol evidence may be admitted to show fraud, duress, mistake, ambiguity, lack of consideration, a condition precedent, or later modifications.
rules.application_example: A party may introduce evidence that the writing was induced by fraud.
rules.common_trap: Later agreements are not barred because the rule covers only prior or contemporaneous evidence.

rules.title: Course of Performance, Course of Dealing, and Usage of Trade
rules.prompt_question: How does the UCC interpret contract terms?
rules.rule_statement: Under the UCC, course of performance, course of dealing, and usage of trade may be used to explain or supplement contract terms.
rules.application_example: Repeated acceptance of late deliveries may help explain the parties' understanding.
rules.common_trap: These concepts can supplement terms even where the writing seems sparse.

rules.title: Ambiguity
rules.prompt_question: How do courts treat ambiguity in contract language?
rules.rule_statement: If contract language is ambiguous, courts may consider admissible extrinsic evidence to determine the parties' intent.
rules.application_example: If a term like chicken has more than one reasonable meaning, surrounding circumstances may be considered.
rules.common_trap: You must identify actual ambiguity before turning to broader interpretive tools.

rules.title: Conditions
rules.prompt_question: What is a condition in contract law?
rules.rule_statement: A condition is an event, not certain to occur, that must occur before a performance obligation becomes due unless its nonoccurrence is excused.
keywords: must occur before performance becomes due
rules.application_example: A requirement that a buyer obtain financing before purchase is a condition precedent.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse a condition with a mere promise.

rules.title: Condition Precedent
rules.prompt_question: What is a condition precedent?
rules.rule_statement: A condition precedent is an event that must occur before a duty to perform arises.
rules.application_example: Payment due only if goods arrive by a certain date is subject to a condition precedent.
rules.common_trap: If the condition does not occur and is not excused, the duty never matures.

rules.title: Concurrent Conditions
rules.prompt_question: What are concurrent conditions?
rules.rule_statement: Concurrent conditions are performances that are due at the same time, so each party's duty is conditioned on the other's readiness and willingness to perform.
rules.application_example: At a closing, the buyer's payment and seller's transfer are usually concurrent conditions.
rules.common_trap: A party who is not ready to perform cannot place the other in breach for nonperformance.

rules.title: Substantial Performance
rules.prompt_question: When does substantial performance satisfy a contract?
rules.rule_statement: Under common law, substantial performance satisfies the contract and triggers the other party's duty to perform, subject to damages for the defects.
rules.application_example: A builder who completes almost all promised work with minor defects may recover the contract price minus damages.
rules.common_trap: Substantial performance usually does not apply where exact performance is expressly required or where defects are material.

rules.title: Material Breach
rules.prompt_question: What is a material breach?
rules.rule_statement: A material breach is a substantial failure of performance that permits the nonbreaching party to withhold its own performance and sue for damages.
rules.application_example: Failure to deliver the main subject of the contract is usually material.
rules.common_trap: Not every breach is material.

rules.title: Perfect Tender Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the perfect tender rule under the UCC?
rules.rule_statement: Under the UCC, a buyer may reject goods if the goods or the tender fail in any respect to conform to the contract, subject to the seller's right to cure and other limits.
rules.application_example: If goods arrive with wrong color or quantity, the buyer may reject them under the perfect tender rule.
rules.common_trap: This rule is broader than common law substantial performance, but it has important qualifications.

rules.title: Seller's Right to Cure
rules.prompt_question: When may a seller cure a nonconforming tender?
rules.rule_statement: A seller may cure a nonconforming tender within the contract time for performance and, in some cases, after the time if the seller had reasonable grounds to believe the tender would be acceptable.
rules.application_example: A seller who ships the wrong goods early may still cure by sending conforming goods before the deadline.
rules.common_trap: Do not stop at buyer's rejection without analyzing cure.

rules.title: Installment Contract
rules.prompt_question: How is breach analyzed in an installment contract under the UCC?
rules.rule_statement: In an installment contract, the buyer may reject a nonconforming installment only if the nonconformity substantially impairs that installment and cannot be cured, and the whole contract is breached only if the nonconformity substantially impairs the value of the entire contract.
rules.application_example: Minor defects in one shipment may not justify canceling the whole installment contract.
rules.common_trap: Do not apply ordinary perfect tender rules mechanically to installment contracts.

rules.title: Anticipatory Repudiation
rules.prompt_question: What is anticipatory repudiation?
rules.rule_statement: Anticipatory repudiation occurs when a party unequivocally indicates before performance is due that the party will not perform.
rules.application_example: A seller's clear statement that it will never deliver next month's goods is anticipatory repudiation.
rules.common_trap: The statement must be clear and unequivocal.

rules.title: Adequate Assurances Under UCC
rules.prompt_question: When may a party demand adequate assurances under the UCC?
rules.rule_statement: When reasonable grounds for insecurity arise, a party may demand adequate assurances of due performance and may suspend performance if commercially reasonable until assurances are received.
rules.application_example: If a buyer hears credible rumors of the seller's insolvency, the buyer may demand assurances.
rules.common_trap: Insecurity is not the same as repudiation, but failure to provide assurances can become repudiation.

rules.title: Retraction of Repudiation
rules.prompt_question: When may a repudiating party retract repudiation?
rules.rule_statement: A repudiating party may retract repudiation until the other party materially changes position, indicates the repudiation is final, or files suit.
rules.application_example: A seller who retracts quickly before the buyer relies may restore the contract.
rules.common_trap: Once the nonbreaching party has relied or treated repudiation as final, retraction is too late.

rules.title: Third Party Beneficiary
rules.prompt_question: When may a third party enforce a contract as a beneficiary?
rules.rule_statement: A third party may enforce a contract when the contract was intended to benefit that third party and the third party's rights have vested.
rules.application_example: If A contracts with B to pay C, C may enforce if C was an intended beneficiary and rights vested.
rules.common_trap: Incidental beneficiaries cannot sue on the contract.

rules.title: Intended vs Incidental Beneficiaries
rules.prompt_question: Who may enforce a contract as a beneficiary?
rules.rule_statement: Only an intended beneficiary, not an incidental beneficiary, may enforce the contract.
rules.application_example: A neighboring store that benefits indirectly from a construction contract is usually only an incidental beneficiary.
rules.common_trap: A benefit alone does not make someone intended.

rules.title: Assignment
rules.prompt_question: What is an assignment?
rules.rule_statement: An assignment is a transfer of contractual rights from the assignor to the assignee.
rules.application_example: A lender may assign its right to receive payment to another party.
rules.common_trap: Assignment transfers rights, not duties.

rules.title: Limits on Assignment
rules.prompt_question: When is assignment ineffective or restricted?
rules.rule_statement: Assignment may be ineffective if it materially changes the obligor's duty or risk, is prohibited by law, or is validly barred by contract or public policy.
rules.application_example: A personal services right generally cannot be assigned if it materially changes performance expectations.
rules.common_trap: Many payment rights are freely assignable, but personal performance expectations are different.

rules.title: Delegation
rules.prompt_question: What is delegation of duties?
rules.rule_statement: Delegation is the transfer of a contractual duty to perform from one party to another, but the delegating party usually remains liable absent novation.
rules.application_example: A builder may delegate ordinary construction duties, but may still remain liable if the delegate fails.
rules.common_trap: Delegation does not by itself release the original obligor.

rules.title: Nondelegable Duties
rules.prompt_question: What duties generally may not be delegated?
rules.rule_statement: Duties involving special skill, personal judgment, or a contract term prohibiting delegation generally may not be delegated.
rules.application_example: A famous singer's performance duty usually cannot be delegated to another singer.
rules.common_trap: Personal service obligations are common exam examples of nondelegable duties.

rules.title: Novation
rules.prompt_question: What is a novation?
rules.rule_statement: A novation is a substituted contract in which all necessary parties agree that a new party will replace an original party and the original party is released.
rules.application_example: If all parties agree that a new buyer replaces the old buyer and releases the old buyer, there is a novation.
rules.common_trap: Without actual agreement to release, there is delegation but not novation.

rules.title: Expectation Damages
rules.prompt_question: What are expectation damages?
rules.rule_statement: Expectation damages put the injured party in the position the party would have occupied had the contract been fully performed.
keywords: position had the contract been fully performed
rules.application_example: A seller denied payment may recover the benefit of the bargain.
rules.common_trap: The goal is to protect expectancy, not punish the breaching party.

rules.title: Consequential Damages
rules.prompt_question: When are consequential damages recoverable?
rules.rule_statement: Consequential damages are recoverable if they were reasonably foreseeable to the breaching party at the time of contract formation and can be proven with reasonable certainty.
rules.application_example: Lost profits from a shutdown may be recoverable if the seller knew delay would halt operations.
rules.common_trap: Foreseeability is judged at formation, not breach.

rules.title: Incidental Damages
rules.prompt_question: What are incidental damages?
rules.rule_statement: Incidental damages are reasonable costs incurred in dealing with the breach, such as expenses of inspection, transportation, care, or arranging a substitute transaction.
rules.application_example: A buyer's shipping and storage costs after seller's breach may be incidental damages.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse incidental damages with consequential damages.

rules.title: Certainty Limitation
rules.prompt_question: What is the certainty limitation on damages?
rules.rule_statement: Contract damages must be proven with reasonable certainty and may not be based on pure speculation.
rules.application_example: A new business may have difficulty proving lost profits if the amount is too speculative.
rules.common_trap: Some uncertainty in amount is tolerated, but not total speculation.

rules.title: Mitigation
rules.prompt_question: What is the mitigation rule?
rules.rule_statement: An injured party may not recover damages that could have been avoided without undue risk, burden, or humiliation.
rules.application_example: A wrongfully discharged employee must make reasonable efforts to find similar employment.
rules.common_trap: The nonbreaching party need not take unreasonable or humiliating substitute work.

rules.title: Liquidated Damages
rules.prompt_question: When is a liquidated damages clause enforceable?
rules.rule_statement: A liquidated damages clause is enforceable if damages were difficult to estimate at formation and the stipulated amount is a reasonable forecast of probable loss.
rules.application_example: A clause setting a fair amount for delay damages in construction may be enforceable.
rules.common_trap: A penalty clause is unenforceable.

rules.title: Specific Performance
rules.prompt_question: When is specific performance available?
rules.rule_statement: Specific performance is available when legal damages are inadequate, such as for unique goods or real property, and equitable relief is otherwise appropriate.
rules.application_example: A buyer of a unique parcel of land may obtain specific performance.
rules.common_trap: Specific performance is usually unavailable for personal service contracts.

rules.title: Injunction in Personal Services Context
rules.prompt_question: When may an injunction be used in a personal services contract case?
rules.rule_statement: Although courts generally will not order specific performance of personal services, they may sometimes enforce a valid negative covenant by injunction if equitable requirements are met.
rules.application_example: A court may enjoin a performer from working for a competitor if a valid exclusivity covenant exists.
rules.common_trap: Courts do not force ongoing personal labor directly.

rules.title: Reliance Damages
rules.prompt_question: What are reliance damages?
rules.rule_statement: Reliance damages reimburse expenses incurred in reliance on the contract by putting the injured party in the position occupied before the contract was made.
rules.application_example: A party who incurred preparation costs in reliance may recover those costs if expectancy is uncertain.
rules.common_trap: Reliance is not meant to produce a better position than full performance would have given.

rules.title: Restitution as Remedy
rules.prompt_question: What is restitution as a contract remedy?
rules.rule_statement: Restitution restores to the claimant the value of benefits conferred on the other party to prevent unjust enrichment.
rules.application_example: A breaching party may sometimes recover restitution for net benefits conferred beyond the other side's damages, subject to limits.
rules.common_trap: Restitution focuses on benefit to the defendant, not the plaintiff's expectation.

rules.title: Identification of Goods
rules.prompt_question: When are goods identified to the contract?
rules.rule_statement: Goods are identified to the contract when they are designated as the goods to which the contract refers.
rules.application_example: Specific existing goods are identified at contract formation; future goods are identified later when designated.
rules.common_trap: Risk issues often depend on identification.

rules.title: Risk of Loss in Shipment Contracts
rules.prompt_question: When does risk of loss pass in a shipment contract?
rules.rule_statement: In a shipment contract, risk of loss passes to the buyer when the seller duly delivers the goods to the carrier.
rules.application_example: If the seller properly ships conforming goods, risk usually shifts when the carrier receives them.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse shipment contracts with destination contracts.

rules.title: Risk of Loss in Destination Contracts
rules.prompt_question: When does risk of loss pass in a destination contract?
rules.rule_statement: In a destination contract, risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are tendered at the destination.
rules.application_example: If the contract requires delivery to buyer's warehouse, risk remains with seller until tender there.
rules.common_trap: Destination language changes the normal shipment rule.

rules.title: Implied Warranty of Merchantability
rules.prompt_question: When does the implied warranty of merchantability arise?
rules.rule_statement: The implied warranty of merchantability arises in a sale of goods if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind and warrants that the goods are fit for ordinary purposes.
rules.application_example: A merchant seller of appliances warrants that the appliances will function for ordinary household use.
rules.common_trap: A casual nonmerchant seller usually does not give this warranty.

rules.title: Implied Warranty of Fitness for Particular Purpose
rules.prompt_question: When does the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose arise?
rules.rule_statement: This warranty arises when the seller has reason to know of the buyer's particular purpose and that the buyer is relying on the seller's skill or judgment to select suitable goods.
keywords: relying on seller's skill or judgment
rules.application_example: If a buyer tells the seller the paint must withstand marine exposure and relies on the seller's recommendation, this warranty may arise.
rules.common_trap: The purpose must be particular, not merely ordinary.

rules.title: Express Warranty
rules.prompt_question: How is an express warranty created?
rules.rule_statement: An express warranty is created by an affirmation of fact, promise, description, or sample that becomes part of the basis of the bargain.
rules.application_example: A seller's statement that a machine produces 500 units per hour may create an express warranty.
rules.common_trap: Puffery or opinion usually does not create an express warranty.

rules.title: Disclaimer of Implied Warranties
rules.prompt_question: How may implied warranties be disclaimed?
rules.rule_statement: Implied warranties may be disclaimed by language meeting UCC requirements, such as mention of merchantability for that warranty, or by expressions like as is, subject to statutory rules.
rules.application_example: A conspicuous as is clause may disclaim implied warranties.
rules.common_trap: Express warranties are harder to disclaim inconsistently.

rules.title: Battle of the Forms
rules.prompt_question: How are additional or different terms treated in a UCC battle of the forms?
rules.rule_statement: Under the UCC, a definite and seasonable expression of acceptance can form a contract despite additional or different terms, and between merchants additional terms may become part of the contract unless an exception applies.
rules.application_example: A buyer's purchase order and seller's acknowledgment with extra boilerplate may still form a contract.
rules.common_trap: Do not apply the common law mirror image rule to UCC goods cases.

rules.title: Accord and Satisfaction
rules.prompt_question: What is accord and satisfaction?
rules.rule_statement: An accord is an agreement to accept substituted performance in discharge of an existing duty, and satisfaction is the performance of that accord.
rules.application_example: A creditor may agree to accept a lesser amount plus early payment in satisfaction of a disputed debt.
rules.common_trap: The original duty is discharged only when the accord is satisfied unless the parties agree otherwise.

rules.title: Discharge by Novation
rules.prompt_question: How does novation discharge contractual obligations?
rules.rule_statement: A novation discharges the original duty when the parties agree that a new obligor or new contract replaces the original one.
rules.application_example: If a creditor agrees to release the old debtor and accept a new debtor instead, the original duty is discharged.
rules.common_trap: Without actual release, there is no novation discharge.

rules.title: Buyer's Cover
rules.prompt_question: What is cover?
rules.rule_statement: After the seller's breach, a buyer may cover by making a good faith and reasonable substitute purchase and recover the difference between the cover price and contract price plus incidental and consequential damages, less expenses saved.
keywords: difference between cover and contract price
rules.application_example: If the seller breaches, the buyer may buy replacement goods and recover the extra cost.
rules.common_trap: Cover is optional, but if the buyer covers unreasonably recovery may be limited.

rules.title: Buyer's Market Damages
rules.prompt_question: What damages may a buyer recover if the buyer does not cover?
rules.rule_statement: A buyer may recover the difference between the market price at the time the buyer learned of the breach and the contract price, plus incidental and consequential damages, less expenses saved.
rules.application_example: If replacement goods were not purchased, the buyer may use market damages instead.
rules.common_trap: Use the correct time and place for measuring market price.

rules.title: Seller's Resale Damages
rules.prompt_question: What damages may a seller recover after resale?
rules.rule_statement: A seller who resells in good faith and in a commercially reasonable manner may recover the difference between the resale price and contract price plus incidental damages, less expenses saved.
keywords: difference between resale and contract price
rules.application_example: A seller may resell rejected goods and recover any loss on the resale.
rules.common_trap: The resale must be commercially reasonable.

rules.title: Seller's Market Damages
rules.prompt_question: What market damages may a seller recover?
rules.rule_statement: A seller may recover the difference between the market price at the time and place for tender and the unpaid contract price, plus incidental damages, less expenses saved.
rules.application_example: If the buyer wrongfully repudiates and the seller does not resell, seller market damages may apply.
rules.common_trap: Measure at time and place for tender, not some later date.

rules.title: Lost Volume Seller
rules.prompt_question: Who is a lost volume seller and what remedy applies?
rules.rule_statement: A lost volume seller is one who could have made both the breached sale and the resale, and may recover lost profits when ordinary resale or market damages are inadequate.
rules.application_example: A car dealer with ample inventory may recover lost profits after buyer breach even though the car is later resold.
rules.common_trap: Resale does not always eliminate the seller's loss.

rules.title: Buyer's Right to Recover Identified Goods
rules.prompt_question: When may a buyer recover identified goods?
rules.rule_statement: A buyer may in some circumstances recover identified goods, including by replevin or specific performance, when cover is unavailable or the goods are unique or specially situated.
rules.application_example: A buyer of rare goods unavailable elsewhere may seek specific recovery.
rules.common_trap: The buyer is not limited to ordinary money damages in every goods case.
